description: Letter from a ḥaver of the Jerusalem yeshiva, identified by Frenkel as probably Yaʿaqov b. Yosef the leader of the community of Aleppo, probably in Aleppo, to a gaʾon, probably Shelomo b. Yehuda, in Jerusalem. Dating: ca. 1035 (Gil) or late 1020s (Frenkel). The sender complains about the members of his congregation who plot against him and have twice slandered him, once to the present gaʾon, once to his predecessor. The sender's innocence has been proven before, and will be proven again, with the help of Ṣadoq b. Levi, whose previous letter was lost. Also mentions Tamīm [b.?] Avraham and Yefet Ibn al-Shayzarī. The writer is also on good terms with the local governor. The writer is leaving for Kalne (Calneh) to attempt to resolve a dispute within the Jewish community there; a man named Ibn al-Lādhiqī (potentially identical with Yehoshuaʿ al-Lādhiqī or his son ʿAlī, cf. T-S 24.59 (PGPID 1808)) is seeking to succeed their recently deceased judge. The general consensus in Geniza scholarship (Mann, Gil, and Frenkel) is that Kalne is al-Raqqa. Others have proposed that Kalne is the village near Aleppo now known as Kullan-Köy. As the recipient’s last letter was lost, the writer requests that the recipient send a letter of support to strengthen the writer’s authority within the community in Aleppo; with allusions to Job 30:26, 10:22 and Proverbs 27:19. (Information in part from CUDL.) MR/ASE
